stem: On the interval \( [0, 2\pi) \), which of the following is a solution of \( [[A]]\cos^2 x + ([[B]])\cos x + ([[C]]) = 0 \)?
solution: Let \( u = \cos x \). The equation \( [[A]]u^2 + ([[B]])u + ([[C]]) = 0 \) factors into rational roots \( u \); solving \( \cos x = u \) on \( [0, 2\pi) \) yields the solution \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{6} \) is irrational, so it cannot be a root of the rational-coefficient quadratic; it is not a solution.
The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \) is irrational, so this angle does not satisfy the equation.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \), landing on an angle whose cosine is not a root.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{3\pi}{4} \), so its cosine no longer equals a root.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{5\pi}{6} \), giving a non-solution.
The cosine of \( \tfrac{3\pi}{4} \) is irrational, so this angle is not a solution.

stem: On the interval \( [0, 2\pi) \), which of the following is a solution of \( [[A]]\sin^2 x + ([[B]])\sin x + ([[C]]) = 0 \)?
solution: Let \( u = \sin x \). The equation \( [[A]]u^2 + ([[B]])u + ([[C]]) = 0 \) factors into rational roots \( u \); solving \( \sin x = u \) on \( [0, 2\pi) \) yields the solution \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: The sine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{3} \) is irrational, so it cannot be a root of the rational-coefficient quadratic.
The sine of \( \tfrac{2\pi}{3} \) is irrational, so this angle does not satisfy the equation.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \), landing on an angle whose sine is not a root.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \), so its sine no longer equals a root.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{3} \), giving a non-solution.
The sine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \) is irrational, so this angle is not a solution.

stem: On the interval \( \left(-\dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \), which of the following is a solution of \( [[A]]\tan^2 x + ([[B]])\tan x + ([[C]]) = 0 \)? (Factor the quadratic in \( \tan x \).)
solution: Let \( u = \tan x \). The equation \( [[A]]u^2 + ([[B]])u + ([[C]]) = 0 \) factors with roots \( u=[[p]] \) and \( u=[[q]] \). Since \( \tan x \) is one-to-one on \( \left(-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \), solving \( \tan x = [[p]] \) or \( \tan x=[[q]] \) there gives \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two arctangents instead of reporting a root by itself.
This has the wrong sign.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \).
This shifts a true solution by \( -\tfrac{\pi}{4} \).
This takes the arctangent of the product of the two roots instead of a root itself.
This takes the arctangent of the sum of the two roots instead of a root itself.

stem: On the interval \( [0, 2\pi) \), which of the following is a solution of \( [[P]]\sin x\cos x + ([[Q]])\sin x = 0 \)? (Factor out the common term.)
solution: Factor out \( \sin x \): \( \sin x\,([[P]]\cos x + [[Q]]) = 0 \). One branch gives \( \sin x = 0 \) and the other gives \( \cos x = -\dfrac{[[Q]]}{[[P]]} \), a nice value; solving on \( [0,2\pi) \) gives (among others) \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{6} \) is irrational and does not satisfy either factor.
The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \) is irrational and does not satisfy either factor.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{6} \), landing off both branches.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \), landing off both branches.
This is \( \tfrac{3\pi}{2} \), which satisfies neither factor for these coefficients.
This is \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \), which satisfies neither factor for these coefficients.
